blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْقَارِعَةُ (١)
The Catastrophe! (1)
مَا ٱلْقَارِعَةُ (٢)
What is the Catastrophe? (2)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْقَارِعَةُ (٣)
What will show you what is the Catastrophe? (3)
يَوْمَ يَكُونُ ٱلنَّاسُ كَٱلْفَرَاشِ ٱلْمَبْثُوثِ (٤)
[It is] the day when mankind will be like scattered moths (4)
وَتَكُونُ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَٱلْعِهْنِ ٱلْمَنفُوشِ (٥)
and the mountains like carded wool. (5)
فَأَمَّا مَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ (٦)
As for him, whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, (6)
فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ (٧)
he will have a pleasing life. (7)
وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ (٨)
But as for him, whose deeds weigh light in the scales, (8)
فَأُمُّهُۥ هَاوِيَةٌ (٩)
his home will be the Abyss. (9)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا هِيَهْ (١٠)
And what will show you what it is? (10)
نَارٌ حَامِيَةٌۢ (١١)
It is a scorching fire! (11)

(Commentary)

This Surah is a warning (إنذار) and a glad tiding (تبشير) regarding the Resurrection (القيامة), though the aspect of warning predominates. The Surah is Meccan.

His saying, the Exalted: الْقارِعَةُ مَا الْقارِعَةُ The Catastrophe! What is the Catastrophe? (101:1-2). These are a subject and a predicate. The term al-Qari‘ah (القارعة) is derived from al-qar‘ (القرع), which refers to striking with great force. It is one of the names of the Resurrection (القيامة) in the Quran. It is said that it was so named because it strikes the hearts with terror and strikes the enemies of Allah with punishment.

The question regarding the reality of the Catastrophe in His saying: مَا الْقارِعَةُ (What is the Catastrophe?), despite it being known, is an indication of the magnitude (تعظيم) and gravity (تفخيم) of its affair, and that its true nature cannot be fully encompassed by knowledge. This magnification and glorification are further emphasized by His subsequent saying: وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْقارِعَةُ And what will show you what the Catastrophe is? (101:3).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَراشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ [It is] the day when mankind will be like scattered moths (101:4). This is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) related to an omitted verb such as "remember," "it strikes," or "it comes." According to what has been narrated from al-Farra’, al-farash (الفراش) refers to locusts that spread out and mount one another—the swarming of locusts. It is said that people at the time of the Resurrection (البعث) are likened to moths because when moths take flight, they do not head in a single direction like other birds; similarly, when people emerge from their graves, terror encompasses them, and they head in various directions, or they head toward their different abodes of felicity or wretchedness. Al-mabthuth (المبثوث) is derived from al-bath (البث), which means scattering or dispersal.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ and the mountains will be like carded wool. (101:5). Al-‘ihn (العهن) is wool of various colors. Al-manfush (المنفوش) is derived from al-nafsh (النفش), which is the spreading of wool by carding or similar means. Thus, "carded wool" (العهن المنفوش) refers to scattered wool of various colors, indicating the disintegration of the mountains—despite their varying colors—by the earthquake of the Hour.

His saying, the Exalted: فأَمَّا مَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ As for him whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, he will have a pleasing life. (101:6-7). This is an allusion to the weighing of deeds (وزن الأعمال). Among deeds are those that are heavy in the scale (الميزان)—meaning they possess value and status with Allah—such as faith (الإيمان) and various types of acts of obedience (الطاعات). Others are not so, such as disbelief (الكفر) and various types of sins (المعاصي). These two categories differ in their consequences: the heavy leads to felicity (السعادة), while the light leads to wretchedness (الشقاء). The discussion regarding the meaning of the scale (الميزان) — the divine standard for measuring the reality and value of human deeds — has already preceded in the commentary of previous Surahs.

His saying: فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ (in a pleasing life): al-‘ishah (العيشة), with a kasrah on the letter ‘ayn, is a noun of state (بناء نوع) like al-jilsah (the manner of sitting). Describing the life as "pleasing" (راضية)—while it is actually the one living it who is pleased—is a form of intellectual trope (المجاز العقلي), or the meaning is "a life possessed of satisfaction."

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُمُّهُ هاوِيَةٌ But as for him whose deeds weigh light in the scales, his home will be the Abyss. (101:8-9). It is apparent that what is meant by hawiyah (هاوية) is Hell (جهنم). It is named hawiyah (the Abyss) because of the falling (هوي) of whoever is cast into it—that is, his descent into the lowest of the low (أسفل سافلين). The Exalted said: ثُمَّ رَدَدْناهُ أَسْفَلَ سافِلِينَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — then We relegated him to the lowest of the low, except those who have faith (95:5-6).

Thus, describing the Fire as hawiyah (the falling/abyss) is an intellectual trope (مجاز عقلي), similar to describing the life as "pleasing." It is called a "mother" (أم) for the one who enters it because it is his abode (مأواه) and his destination (مرجعه) to which he returns, just as a child returns to his mother.

It has also been said that "his mother" (أمه) refers to the crown of his head (أم رأسه), and the meaning is that the crown of his head shall fall (هاوية) into it—meaning he falls into it headlong because they are cast into the Fire on their heads. However, this is rendered unlikely by the pronoun in His saying: ما هِيَهْ (what it is), which would be left without a clear referent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَدْراكَ ما هِيَهْ And what will show you what it is? (101:10). The pronoun "it" (هي) refers to the Abyss (هاوية). The letter ha’ (الهاء) in هِيَهْ is for pausing (الوقف). The sentence is an explanation intended to magnify the matter of the Fire and emphasize its gravity.

His saying, the Exalted: نارٌ حامِيَةٌ A blazing fire! (101:11). Meaning, hot and intensely so. This is the answer to the question in ما هِيَهْ (what it is) and an explanation of the Abyss (هاوية).

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ, he said: "Al-‘ihn (العهن) is wool." Regarding His saying: وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ, he said: "Of good deeds." Regarding His saying: فأُمُّهُ هاوِيَةٌ, he said: "The crown of his head; he is hurled into the Fire on his head."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: إن نفس المؤمن إذا قبضت ـ يلقاها أهل الرحمة من عباد الله ـ كما يلقون البشير من أهل الدنيا فيقولون : انظروا صاحبكم يستريح فإنه كان في كرب شديد ـ ثم يسألونه ما فعل فلان وفلانة؟ هل تزوجت؟ فإذا سألوه عن الرجل قد مات قبله فيقول : هيهات قد مات ذاك قبلي فيقولون : إنا لله وإنا إليه راجعون ذهب به إلى أمه الهاوية ـ فبئست الأم وبئست المربية. — "When the soul of a believer is taken, the people of mercy among the servants of Allah meet it just as they meet a bringer of glad tidings in the world. They say: 'Look at your companion, let him rest, for he was in great distress.' Then they ask him: 'What did so-and-so (man) and so-and-so (woman) do? Did she marry?' If they ask him about a man who had died before him, he says: 'Alas, that one died before me.' They then say: 'Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we do return; he has been taken to his mother, the Abyss (الهاوية)—and what a wretched mother and what a wretched nurturer!'"

I say: This meaning has also been narrated from Anas bin Malik, and from al-Hasan and al-Ash‘ath bin ‘Abdullah al-A‘ma, from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.
